items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: How does a buffer maintain its pH when small amounts of acid are added?
items.correct: The conjugate base in the buffer reacts with added H+ to remove it, keeping pH nearly constant.
items.misconception: A buffer prevents any H+ ions from being added to the solution at all.
items.random_wrong: A buffer destroys added acid by changing it into water vapour.
items.why: Students treat 'buffer' as a magical pH-locker rather than a chemical equilibrium that absorbs H+ or OH- through reactions with its components.

items.q: Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
items.correct: Each successive element has an additional electron shell, increasing the distance of outer electrons from the nucleus.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger down a group because they have more protons attracting more matter.
items.random_wrong: Atoms get bigger down a group because nuclei swell when they have more neutrons.
items.why: Students attribute size to mass/protons (a 'more stuff = bigger' intuition) rather than to the number of shells.
It's Al2O3. “Na2O” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate pH of SO3 dissolved in water?
items.why: Correct answer: ~0. “~7” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: pH of 0.1 mol/dm3 strong base NaOH?
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
